coredata.dc:title: An efficient feature search approach for robust state of health estimation of Li-ion battery
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Beijing Institute of Technology Press Co., Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Beijing Institute of Technology Press Co., Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xiaolong
Yang, Huihui
Yang, Yingze
Li, Heng
Chen, Yuan
Zhang, Chaolong
Wu, Longxing
Yan, Lisen
coredata.dc:description: Accurate state of health (SOH) estimation is fundamental in battery prognostics and health management. Existing estimation methods often rely on manually selected features, limiting their generalizability and robustness due to subjective bias. This paper proposes an efficient feature search method for Li-ion batteries in multidimensional feature space, such as Cycle-Voltage-SOC, according to feature self-volatility and correlation with battery capacity. Bayesian optimization is applied to search for optimal features efficiently. With the searched features, a stacking ensemble regression model is developed to estimate the SOH of batteries. The proposed method provides interpretable feature extraction and improves the accuracy of the SOH estimation. Experimental results demonstrate the efficiency of the proposed feature search method and the SOH estimation model.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Feature engineering
Battery aging feature
State of health
Bayesian optimization
Ensemble robust estimation model

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Bayesian Optimization for search feature index
Search optimal index of the feature
Ensemble robust model to estimate SOH

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic of the proposed feature index search framework for robust SOH estimation.
The feature curves of a battery at different cycles. (a) Voltage-SOC. (b) Voltage-IC.
The different trends on the feature surface under different index reflect the battery aging. (a) Cycle-SOC-Voltage. (b) Cycle-Voltage-IC.
Bayesian optimization process for extracting optimal features.
The proposed ensemble robust model.
Voltage, current and temperature of the battery under complete charge/discharge cycles. (a) Scatter plot of the complete data. (b) Scatter plot of the cleaned data.
Comparison of IC curves after linear interpolation and s-g filtering.
Resampling comparison scatter plots of constant current charging IC curves. (a) Raw data. (b) Data after filtering and resampling.

Feature indexing information learned by Bayesian optimized agent models. (a) Cycle-SOC-Voltage, (c) Cycle-Voltage-SOC, and (e) Cycle-Voltage-IC are obtained from the charging process, while (b) Cycle-SOC-Voltage, (d) Cycle-Voltage-SOC, and (f) Cycle-Voltage-IC represent the discharging process.

Optimal feature index and feature interval of the battery obtained by Bayesian optimization search. (a) Cycle-SOC-Voltage, (c) Cycle-Voltage-SOC, and (e) Cycle-Voltage-IC are the search results for the charging process. (b) Cycle-SOC-Voltage, (d) Cycle-Voltage-SOC, and (f) Cycle-Voltage-IC are the search results for the discharge process.

Comparative analysis of trends in normalized battery capacity and different point features. The horizontal coordinate is the number of cycle rounds of battery operation, and the vertical coordinate indicates different normalized data. (a) Maximum IC value when discharged and IC value with voltage decreased to 3.51 V. (b) SoC at the end of CC charging versus when the voltage rises to 4.09 V.

Comparative trend analysis of nominalized battery capacity and features of different intervals. (a) Area of the Voltage-SoC curve: Complete CC charging process and BO to obtain a voltage interval of [4.02, 4.14]. (b) Time of is voltage change: Empirically based voltage intervals [3.5, 4.2] and [4.02, 4.14] obtained by BO.
SOH obtained by searching the area of the IC curve in the voltage interval and the area near the peak of the IC curve as feature.
SOH comparison of downscaled features of voltage data under SoC intervals obtained by complete charging and BO searching.
The correlation coefficient and information coefficient between different features and capacity.
The SOH estimation and error analysis with the empirical and the proposed features on different battery. (a) B0005. (a) B0005. (b) B0007. (d) B0018.

Boxplots of absolute errors in SOH estimations. The green and blue boxes represent the error distributions when estimating using the empirical features and using the features from this paper, respectively.
The Lithium-ion battery aging data profiles.
Battery optimal feature index.
Data segmentation strategy.
The comparison of battery SOH estimation.

head.ce:title: An efficient feature search approach for robust state of health estimation of Li-ion battery

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate state of health (SOH) estimation is fundamental in battery prognostics and health management. Existing estimation methods often rely on manually selected features, limiting their generalizability and robustness due to subjective bias. This paper proposes an efficient feature search method for Li-ion batteries in multidimensional feature space, such as Cycle-Voltage-SOC, according to feature self-volatility and correlation with battery capacity. Bayesian optimization is applied to search for optimal features efficiently. With the searched features, a stacking ensemble regression model is developed to estimate the SOH of batteries. The proposed method provides interpretable feature extraction and improves the accuracy of the SOH estimation. Experimental results demonstrate the efficiency of the proposed feature search method and the SOH estimation model.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries are widely used in mobile devices, electric vehicles, and energy storage systems due to their high energy density, no memory effect, and environmental friendliness [1]. However, with the passage of time and frequent use, the battery will inevitably age, resulting in a decline in performance, being unable to meet the needs of use, and even causing safety problems [2]. Therefore, accurate estimation of the battery's state of health (SOH) is essential to delay battery aging, prevent potential safety risks, and ensure the reliable operation of the system.

The existing SOH estimation for Li-ion batteries includes mainly model-based methods, data-driven methods, and hybrid methods [3–5]. Model-based methods estimate SOH by establishing a mathematical model of the battery, but the method relies on the establishment of a mathematical model, which is limited in accuracy and difficult to estimate online. The data-driven approach establishes a mapping relationship between input features and SOH through machine learning, which can efficiently handle complex nonlinear relationships, but requires a large amount of data and is prone to overfitting. Hybrid models utilize the physical basis of the model and the flexibility of data-driven to improve the accuracy and robustness of SOH estimation but are difficult to apply due to the complexity of their models and the large amount of computation. Among these methods, data-driven methods have received widespread attention because they do not rely on complex internal battery mechanisms, can adapt to diverse battery operating conditions, and handle complex nonlinear relationships.

Current research on data-driven SOH estimation can be divided into featureless methods and feature-based methods [6,7]. Featureless methods directly use raw data input to the model, rely on the model's own learning ability, and do not require manual feature extraction. Common methods include Long-Short-Term Memory networks (LSTM), Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), and Gated Recurrent Units (GRU) [8–11]. Featureless methods do not require manual feature extraction, reduce perceived interference, and can capture complex nonlinear relationships. However, they require large amounts of data and computational resources, and the interpretability of the model is relatively poor. Feature-based methods require the extraction of features related to battery aging from battery data and then the data is fed into the estimation model. For example, the key features of the battery are extracted using Variable Model Decomposition (VMD) [12], Whale Optimization Algorithm (WOA) [13], LSTM [14], and then combined with regression methods such as Gaussian Process Regression (GPR) [15,16], GRU [17,18], and Support Vector Regression (SVR) [19] for SOH estimation. Feature-based methods reduce data redundancy and improve model efficiency and accuracy by extracting key features, which are widely used in practical applications.

For feature-based SOH estimation for Li-ion batteries, feature extraction is a key link, and its estimation accuracy largely depends on the quality of the extracted features. Existing feature extraction methods are mainly categorized into point-based and interval-based feature extraction methods. Point-based methods primarily focus on the key points in the battery charge/discharge curve or Electrochemical Impedance Spectra (EIS) [20], aiming to analyze the battery's key aging characteristics through precise positioning. Currently, point-based methods can be divided into three types: first, the point can be characterized from statistical information, such as the maximum value of the measurable data, the extreme value, and so on. For example, in Refs. [21–23], the maximum and extreme value information including the maximum and minimum temperatures is extracted during the charging and discharging process. Secondly, raw data can be processed based on the battery mechanism before extracting features, such as the Differential Thermal Voltammetry (DTV) method [24], the peak value, as well as the half-peak area change of the Incremental Capacity (IC) curve [25,26], and the complex voltage response in the relaxation phase after charging [27]. Both types of methods require complete charge and discharge data to minimize bias. Therefore, some studies have proposed methods to extract features based on feature indexing. Selecting some signal values under the indexes of other features, such as extracting the SOC of the battery as a feature at the end of the constant-current phase in the CC-CV charging process [28]. However, the cutoff voltage is usually set based on human experience, which does not adequately reflect the trend of battery aging.

Interval-based feature extraction methods, conversely, focus on the overall feature variations within a specific voltage interval or time interval during battery charging and discharging to mitigate noise effects. Signal processing-based methods evaluate SOH by analyzing the integral, rate of change, or other statistical features of voltage, current, or temperature over a specific interval. For example, in Ref. [29], features are fused by selecting features within a specific voltage interval and integrating entropy weight methods with correlation factors to reduce redundant information and improve the accuracy of SOH estimation. With the development of data-driven methods, feature extraction methods combined with deep learning techniques have gradually become a research hotspot, such as automatically extracting multidimensional features using CNN [30], reducing continuous data segments to scalar values or low-dimensional vectors by parametric models such as autoencoders [31] or parameter-free algorithms such as PCA, KPCA, LTSA [32]. However, the determination of the action intervals in the existing studies is almost based on human experience, and how to choose the appropriate action intervals is still a current difficulty in feature extraction.

Whether it is point-based feature extraction or interval-based feature extraction, the existing feature extraction methods almost all rely on expert experience, and although these methods can effectively reflect the aging characteristics of batteries to a certain extent, they also have some limitations. First, methods relying on expert experience usually require a large amount of experimental data to verify the validity of the features, which increases the cost of data acquisition and processing. Second, the generalization ability of these methods may be limited under different battery types, aging modes, and operating conditions, making it difficult to adapt to diverse application scenarios. In addition, the subjectivity of expert experience may lead to feature selection bias, affecting the model's accuracy and reliability. Therefore, extracting effective features of batteries without relying on expert experience is difficult.

To address the above issues, a highly efficient feature extraction method is proposed to enable accurate SOH estimation using lightweight prediction models. First, to reduce the dependency on prior knowledge, the search framework of battery feature optimal index in multi-dimensional mapping is established by utilizing Bayesian optimization. Subsequently, an ensemble linear model is employed to avoid the overfitting problem with robustness.

In summary, the main contributions of this paper are as follows:
(1)
A feature effectiveness measurement method is designed, incorporating both the fluctuation of feature vectors and their correlation with battery capacity.
(2)
A Bayesian optimization-based feature extraction framework is constructed to identify optimal feature index, thereby reducing dependence on expert experience. This framework is adaptable to various feature combinations for discovering suitable battery feature index and intervals.
(3)
An ensemble model is developed by stacking Bayesian Ridge Regression and Random Sample Consensus Regression, enhancing the robustness of SOH estimation.
(4)
Extensive experiments are conducted on the proposed feature extraction and SOH estimation methods to validate the effectiveness of the proposed framework and ensemble model.

This paper is organized as follows: The feature extraction framework is presented in Section 2, following the SOH estimation model in Section 3. Experimental results and analysis are demonstrated in Section 4. Section 5 concludes the work.

The schematic of the proposed feature index search framework for robust SOH estimation is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Raw data is first preprocessed to construct a multi-dimensional feature space. Subsequently, optimal feature indexes or index intervals are searched within the feature space by using Bayesian optimization. Online feature extraction is then performed using the optimal feature indexes to provide the inputs for the estimation model, an ensemble that combines a Bayesian Ridge Regressor and a Random Sample Consensus regressor.

For example, the 3D feature space of Cycle–SOC–Voltage can be constructed, where SOC is used as the feature index idx and Voltage is taken as the extracted feature for the estimation model. If idx is set to 0.6, this involves selecting the battery voltage at SOC = 0.6 in each cycle to form the feature vector X across all cycles. The effectiveness of X and idx is evaluated using designed metrics, after which Bayesian optimization is used to find the optimal idx.

Previous research usually analyze battery aging from a feature curve concerning the cycle, as shown in Fig. 2 . Features are generally selected based on intuitive experience. For example, the voltage when SOC = 0.6 in Fig. 2(a) can be selected as a feature, and the maximum value of IC in Fig. 2(b) is selected as a feature, and so on. The method will be limited by human experience when extracting features. For example, the SOC value 0.6 is set artificially, and the corresponding voltage at this SOC value may not necessarily best reflect the battery's aging.

To seek the optimal index, the feature surface is constructed as shown in Fig. 3 . The x, y, and z axes represent Cycle, feature index, and feature, respectively, which allows us to obtain the feature value under different cycles at a specific idx. Fig. 3(a) displays the 3D surface of Cycle–SOC–Voltage. It can be seen that battery aging leads to the voltages along the cycle axes at the same SOC value, such as 0.6, with obvious decrease trends (shown as the red curve in Fig. 3(a)).

Similarly, Fig. 3(b) shows the 3D surface of Cycle-Voltage-IC, demonstrating that as battery aging progresses, IC exhibits diverse trajectories for different voltages and indicates notable differences in trends (shown as the red curves in Fig. 3(b)). Therefore, it is conducive to ensuring the optimal index to let the extracted features best reflect battery aging.

Existing feature engineering methods are usually based on correlation coefficients to evaluate the effectiveness of features. However, correlation coefficients only focus on the linear relationship between the features and the target variables, ignoring the intrinsic properties of the features themselves. To enable a more comprehensive evaluation, a composite metric is used, combining the coefficient of variation (C v), correlation coefficient (ρ), and maximum information coefficient (MIC).

First, C v is employed as one of the metrics to evaluate the dispersion relative to statistics, which makes it reasonable when assessing the degree of fluctuation of features. It can be calculated by Eq. (1).
(1)
C v
(
Y
x i
)
=
σ
(
Y
x i
)
μ
(
Y
x i
)
where
Y
x i
is the sequence of features, x
i is the index value of the feature, and i is the i-th index. σ, μ are the standard deviation and mean of the feature sequence
Y
x i
, respectively.

However, Using C v as a unique metric will pay too much attention to the degree of data dispersion, reducing the diversity of the features. Therefore, the linear correlation between features and capacity is introduced, calculated via Pearson's correlation coefficient (ρ), as defined in Eq. (2).
(2)
ρ
(
Y
x i
, C )
=
(
Y

, C )
σ

)
σ

where Cov(⋅) denotes the covariance function, Var(⋅) is the variance function, and
Y
x i
¯
represents the mean of the variable
Y
x i
. C represents the vector consisting of the capacity values for the corresponding cycle. The covariance is normalized to the range [−1, 1] by dividing by the product of the respective standard deviations,a process that removes the magnitude effect.

However, analyzing linear correlations alone is not sufficient for complex nonlinear systems such as batteries, so this paper introduces the maximum information coefficient (MIC) to measure the linear and nonlinear relationships between features and capacities. MIC is an information measure based on mutual information (MI), which can be viewed as the amount of information contained in one random variable for another random variable, or the amount of information in one random variable due to the known uncertainty of another. The formula for MI is shown in Eq. (3).
(3)
MI
(
Y
x i
, C )
=
∫
y ∈
Y
x i
∫
c ∈ C
p
( y , c )
log 2
P
( y , c )
P
( y )
P
( c )
d y d c
where, P(y, c) is the joint probability between variables y and c. P(y) is the probability that variable y occurs and P(c) is the probability that variable c occurs.

MIC simplifies the solution of joint probability in mutual information by discretizing the data into a two-dimensional space and representing it with a scatterplot, dividing into a and b intervals in the direction of the x-axis and y-axis, respectively, and counting the number of scatter points that fall into each region. The MIC is calculated as follows:
(4)
MIC
(
Y
x i
, C )
=
max
a ∗ b < B
MI
( Y , C )
log 2
min
( a , b )
where a and b denote the number of grids delineated in the x, y directions, which is essentially characterize the grid distribution. B is the hyperparameter, usually set to about 0.6 times the number of data.

Finally, to comprehensively consider the degree of data variation of the feature values themselves, and the linear and nonlinear correlation between the feature vectors and the capacity degradation trend, this paper combines the three metrics, namely the C v, ρ, and MIC coefficient, to obtain the efficient Metric (EM) of the battery features. Since the coefficients C v, ρ, and MIC exhibit different scales and their fluctuations vary across different feature mappings, balancing these three indices requires normalizing them to the [0, 1] range first, followed by weighted summation. The calculation of the feature efficiency index is shown in Eq. (5):
(5)
EM
(
Y
x i
)
=
α ·
C v
(
Y
x i
, C )
+ β · ρ
(
Y
x i
, C ) + γ · MIC
(
Y
x i
, C )
where α, β, and γ are the hyper-parameter weights of the C v, ρ, and MIC coefficient, respectively, which are used to regulate the effects of different coefficients on the aging index, and the sum of α, β, and γ is equal to 1. It should be noted that x
i denotes the feature index, which is a scalar value, and
Y
x i
denotes the eigenvector under the feature index, whereas C is the vector of the battery capacity for each cycle corresponding to
Y
x i
.

By normalizing and weighting the sum of these three metrics, it is possible to balance the effects of each of them, taking into account both the degree of fluctuation in the eigenvalues and the correlation between the features and the battery capacity. Additionly, the hyper-parameter weights can be adjusted to adapt to different battery scenarios and identify the most appropriate battery feature index.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bayesian Optimization for search feature index
ce:section.ce:para: In this paper, the 3D feature mapping constructed via the aforementioned method serves as the search space for features, with the battery feature validity index acting as the objective function to identify the optimal feature index and interval. Given the vast search space due to the continuous nature of feature index values in battery scenarios, we employ Bayesian optimization (BO) to efficiently utilize historical search information and reduce computational costs.

BO is an approximative black-box optimization technique that selects parameters for subsequent experiments based on accumulated historical data. Its decision-making relies on a probabilistic agent model and an acquisition function. The process of BO for extracting the optimal features is shown in Fig. 4 . Specifically, the agent model learns from known feature validity and corresponding vectors, and the acquisition function guides the selection of new feature index values that are most likely to achieve global optimality. This iterative process updates the agent model with new data until a predefined maximum number of iterations or feature validity threshold is reached. Ultimately, BO identifies the optimal feature index and corresponding aging feature interval.

According to the definition of efficient metric, EM(x
i ) denotes the volatility of the eigenvectors under x
i and the similarity with the capacity, which is the aging trend of the battery. Therefore, in this paper, we use EM(x
i ) as the objective function and maximize the objective function by finding the optimal feature index
x
i
∗
as shown in Eq. (6),
(6)
x i ∗
=
arg
max
x i
∈ X
EM
(
Y
x i
)
Algorithm 1
Bayesian optimization (BO) algorithm
Input: Battery feature index ( x
i ∈ ℝ N×1), Battery feature (
Y
x i
∈
ℝ
N × M
) , N is the number of cycles, M is the number of samples in the cycle
Output: Optimal feature index ( i
optim ∈ ℝ1×1)
Initialize:
1. Set optimization targets, i.e. efficientness metrics (EM)
2. Select the feature index x
i and the feature vector Y , and the number of iterations k
3. Select probabilistic surrogate model GPR and acquisition function UCB
4. Select indexes in feature space and initialize GPR
while i ≤ k do
Based on the GPR information, the UCB strategy selects the next experimental index x∗ Get the feature data Y (x∗) ∈ ℝ N×1 with index x∗
Calculate EM(x∗) using Y (x∗) and the battery capacity C
Add new experimental data pair

Specifically, in this paper the Bayesian optimization approach uses [
x i
, EM
(

x i
)
] information pairs to allow the probabilistic agent model to learn the basic mapping relationship between feature index x
i and its corresponding feature
Y

validity, and then, using a collection function, we select the next feature index x
i to be judged from the probabilistic agent model and compute the EM

] information pairs. Agent model. Keep iterating until the termination condition is satisfied. The pseudo-code of its overall process is shown in Algorithm 1.

Since the mapping relationship between the battery feature index xi and its corresponding feature vector validity EM
(
Y
x i
)
is unknown, a model must to be constructed to learn this association. However, using traditional supervised learning methods such as neural networks results in insufficient training samples due to the time-consuming nature of obtaining the label EM
(
Y
x i
)
. Moreover, the single-sample iterative update in Bayesian optimization is prone to causing model instability or catastrophic forgetting. In addition, the parametric model needs to be custom designed for different battery feature spaces and lacks universality.

To address the black-box optimization problem of searching for battery feature indexes, this paper chooses Gaussian process regression (GPR) as a probabilistic agent model to solve the above problems. The nonparametric nature of GPR allows it to adaptively approximate the objective function based on the existing data, and at the same time quantifies the uncertainty by predicting the mean μ and variance σ, which guides the optimization of the sampling process. Moreover, the smoothness assumption of the Gaussian process helps to portray the behavioral pattern of the function in continuous space and enhances the generalization ability. The kernel function measures the variable correlation by squared exponential form, where the parameter l controls the local influence range of the kernel function:
(7)
K
(
x 1
,
x 2
)
= exp
−
‖
x 1
−
x 2
‖ 2 2
2
l 2
where ‖
x
1
−
x
2
‖
2
2
represents the squared L2 norm between x 1 and x 2, which is the Euclidean distance.

In this scenario, suppose the known set of information pairs is {
(
x i
, EM
(

x i
)
)
∣
x i

(

)
is the efficientness of the feature vector
Y
x i
corresponding to the feature index x
i . GPR calculates the covariance matrix K and the covariance vector K ∗ of the to-be-measured point x∗ with the known set X by means of a kernel function, which in turn calculates the mean μ
( EM

and the variance σ
( EM

)
of the validity of the eigenvectors under x∗ based on the joint Gaussian distribution. The joint normal distribution of GPR is shown in Eq. (8):
(8)

where k∗∗ is the variance of the feature index to be tested itself, which in this case is obtained as a value of 1 using the Gaussian kernel function.

Eq. (9) is derived from the properties of the joint Gaussian distribution, where μ∗ and σ∗ represent the mean and variance, respectively, of its eigenvector validity EM
(
Y
x ∗
)
as predicted by the GPR when the feature index is x∗.

When the agent model learns the information, it needs to select a new feature index for further optimization. Whether to choose the index value that makes the expectation of the efficiency of the feature vector larger to be utilized, or to choose the index value that makes the uncertainty of the efficientness of the feature vector larger to be explored is an important factor in improving the efficiency of Bayesian optimization. In Bayesian optimization, the acquisition function is usually used to balance the exploration and utilization.

Based on the information learned from the agent model, a balanced exploration and utilization of the collection function is used to assess the value of each parameter experiment and select the most valuable parameter for the experiment. In this paper, we choose the Upper Confidence Bound (UCB) of the maximum value as the collection function, and the UCB uses the mean and variance of the target to consider utilization and exploration, respectively, and adjusts the ratio of exploration and utilization through the hyper-parameter β , as shown in Eq. (10).
(10)
μ , σ = GPR
(
x i
)
α UCB
(
x i
)
= μ +
β
σ
where x
i is the value of the feature index to be tested, GPR(⋅) is a probabilistic agent model for Gaussian process regression, and α UCB(x
i ) is the value of the feature index to be explored; the higher the value, the more likely x
i is to be used as the value of the feature index to be explored next time.

Therefore, the objective of the acquisition function is as shown in Eq. (11), i.e., to find the feature index point of the optimal value.
(11)
x ∗
=
α UCB
( X )
=
arg
max
x i
∈ X
( μ +
β
σ )

In the battery SOH estimation scenario, outliers, noise in the data, and sensitivity of the model to over-fitting are the main sources of challenges. This paper proposes a robust integrated model to address these issues. Enhancing SOH estimation accuracy and robustness by stacking Bayesian Ridge Regression (BRR) and Randomized Consistent Sampling (RANSAC) regression models. The model takes multiple battery features at a given cycle as input data, and obtains the capacity estimation for that cycle through an integrated learning approach, thus efficiently addressing the challenges of data outliers, noise, and over-fitting.

Bayesian Ridge Regression (BRR) employs Bayesian inference to address parameter uncertainty in regression tasks. By introducing L2 regularization (controlled by parameter λ), BRR mitigates overfitting while balancing bias-variance trade-offs. The method assumes Gaussian priors for regression coefficientsβ, enabling maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimation to optimize:
(12)
β MAP
=
argmax β
( p
( β ∣ X , y )
∝ p
( y ∣ X , β )
p
( β )
)
− λ ‖
β B
‖ 2
where, X is a given input feature matrix, y is the target value vector. β is the vector of regression coefficients, p( y ∣ X , β ) is the conditional probability distribution of y given β and X , and p( β ) is the prior probability distribution of β . ‖ β
B‖2 denotes the square of the L2 norm of the regression coefficient β
B.

The final model incorporates noise ϵ:
(13)
y brr
= β X + ϵ
BRR adapts to nonlinear relationships and noise in battery capacity prediction, enhancing feature weighting and capturing complex feature interactions.

Random Sample Consensus (RANSAC) is a robust regression method that iteratively samples data to exclude outliers and noise. Additionally, RANSAC automatically selects the best-fitting model and filters out poorly contributing samples. For battery SOH estimation, this approach can reduce the model complexity and computational cost while improving prediction accuracy. The core steps of this method include: randomly selecting the minimum data subset to fit an initial model, identifying inliers based on a residual threshold T, and refitting the model using these inliers. The model with the largest number of inliers is ultimately chosen as the optimal solution w∗. The final model is expressed as:
(14)
y ransac
=
ω ∗
X + ϵ

Despite the advantages of BRR and RANSAC in lithium-ion battery SOH estimation, limitations remain. The Gaussian distribution assumption of BRR may introduce overly stringent constraints, which could affect its performance. Meanwhile, the performance of the RANSAC regression method may be influenced by the initial random sampling, resulting in inconsistent iteration outcomes and unstable model selection.

Therefore, we employ a stacking ensemble learning approach to combine the linear regression models of BRR and RANSAC for battery SOH regression prediction. As shown in Fig. 5 , BRR and RANSAC models are first used as base learners, each providing an initial estimation of battery capacity based on their unique strengths. Subsequently, the outputs from these base learners are integrated and aggregated using a linear regression model as the meta-learner. This process aims to leverage the strengths of each model, reduce the bias and variance of individual learners, and ultimately achieve a more accurate and robust estimation of battery capacity.

The model can be represented as:
(15)
y meta
= f
(
y brr
,
y ransac
)
+ b
where, y brr is the prediction result of BRR, y ransac is the prediction result of RANSAC, y meta is the prediction result of the total model, f is a function of the meta-learner, and b is the intercept of the total model.

The experiments were performed on the NASA battery aging dataset. The dataset first needs to be preprocessed before the experiment to add missing values and reduce noise or outliers. The experiment is divided into two main steps: first, the optimal feature index of the battery cell is obtained by using the search framework and the performance is validated. Second, multiple features are input into the proposed model for SOH estimation. Ablation experiments and comparison analysis are conducted to validate both the efficiency of the features and the accuracy of the proposed model.

For the purpose of this paper, we use the NASA battery aging dataset, whose experiments use 18650-type lithium-ion batteries. This dataset was obtained by performing continuous charge/discharge cycles under the charge/discharge protocol of constant-current-constant-voltage(CC-CV) charging and constant-current(CC) discharging, and the basic information of the dataset is shown in Table 1 .

The real collected data has startup noise, jitter noise, and quiescent period, and these irregular information not only affects the performance of the Bayesian optimization algorithm but also seriously affects the estimation model of battery SOH. As shown in Fig. 6 , the voltage, current, and temperature data collected under a single cycle of the battery from the NASA battery aging test dataset are plotted, where the blue curve indicates the CC-CV charging process and the red curve indicates the constant-current discharging process. Startup noise and quiescent periods exhibit significant randomness across cycles, necessitating raw data cleaning to remove noise and quiescent segments. Fig. 6(b) is the curve drawn after cleaning the original data in Fig. 6(a). It can be seen from the figure that the cleaned curve removes the noise and static in the original, reflecting a certain trend.

Furthermore, in addition to the intuitively observable irregularity information, there is also the effect of missing values and outliers within the collected battery data. Smoothing or filtering algorithms can be used to clean the raw data. For data such as voltage and current that are easily affected by acquisition noise, this paper uses Savitzky-Golay (s-g) smoothing to enhance robustness. For data with more important peak information such as IC, this paper uses Gaussian smoothing to preserve the location of the peaks. Fig. 7 shows the comparative results of using linear interpolation and s-g filtering for current and voltage, and Gaussian smoothing for the resulting IC data.

When performing Bayesian optimization, differences in different loops and in the sampling frequency within loops may lead to distortions in the feature vector data obtained through feature indexing, making the optimization results biased towards intervals with a higher frequency of occurrence and affecting the accurate representation of trends. Therefore, in this paper, the original data are resampled in the time dimension based on linear interpolation to ensure that the data intervals are consistent and that the data are aligned on the time axis, which improves the efficiency of Bayesian optimization and the accuracy of the search results. Fig. 8 shows the results of the graphs of IC curves before and after resampling by time compared with the edge histograms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Search optimal index of the feature
ce:section.ce:para: This paper employs Bayesian optimization to search for the optimal aging feature index from the battery feature space and obtain the aging feature index interval. By analyzing the battery features and conducting experiments, we select Cycle-Voltage-IC, Cycle-OCV-SOC and Cycle–SOC–OCV from OCV-SOC, DV, DTV and IC curves for Bayesian optimization, the main reason being that curves such as DTV have large data variation in a single cycle, which makes it difficult to use probabilistic agent models to learn efficient information. In addition, considering that it is impossible to operate the battery from the ideal state, the time scale is not considered for modeling.

The results of the optimal feature index search using BO are shown in Fig. 9 , which demonstrates the validity information of the battery features under each index learned by the probabilistic agent model in BO. The horizontal axis in the figure represents the feature indexes, the vertical axis represents the feature validity after normalization, the scatter represents the true information, the curve represents the mean value predicted by the agent model, and the shaded area represents the uncertainty interval. Finally, we use red pentagrams to indicate the best points obtained from the search. Due to the limitation of text space, Fig. 9 only shows the final results in battery B0006. The full search results are presented in Table 2 .

Through experiments, it was found that the evaluation of feature efficiency involves the value of three subcoefficients. First, the correlation coefficient ρ is crucial for reflecting battery aging trends. If it is not taken into account and features are selected solely based on volatility, the selected results are likely to be influenced by external interference factors such as noise, leading to reduced accuracy. Second, the coefficient of variation C v also plays an important role in the feature selection process. Referring to Fig. 9(a) and (c), and Fig. 9(e), when the battery is in a specific interval before constant voltage charging or during a certain stage of constant current discharging, the correlation between its features and battery capacity approaches 1. This makes it difficult to select the optimal features based solely on correlation. Introducing C v to measure the volatility of feature vectors helps to overcome this limitation and more accurately select feature index. This can be verified by observing Fig. 3(b): when SoC is in the range of 0.5–0.6, although the voltage feature vectors of the battery under different cycles show similar trends, the slight differences in data spans (e.g., at SoC of 0.6, the maximum voltage difference between different cycles is 0.15 V) make it difficult to distinguish based solely on correlation. Here, considering volatility becomes particularly critical.

However, when data contain noise and outliers, these two metrics introduce significant errors in trend and variability calculations, making the inclusion of the MIC coefficient more robust. In addition, it is found that α, β, and γ in the validity index are more appropriately set to 0.2, 0.4, and 0.4, respectively.

In Table 2, the first column lists index names, such as C_SOC_2_Vol indicates that SOC is used as the index to select the battery voltage as the feature data when charging, and D_Vol_2_IC indicates that the battery voltage is used as the index to select the IC value as the feature data when discharging, and the other index names are similar. Columns 2 to 5 list each battery's optimal idx, and the last column indicates the average optimal idx. For example, the second column of row 2 indicates that the voltage of battery B0005 at 53% SOC during charging best reflects its aging features.

Fig. 10 illustrates the optimal feature indexes and index intervals obtained by Bayesian optimization searching in the battery feature space. The black curves represent the feature vectors corresponding to the optimal feature indexes, while the dark green regions represent the intervals suitable for battery feature extraction. As shown in Fig. 10(a) and (b), analysis shows that the end voltage of the battery reflects battery state most obviously when SoC lies in [0.47, 0.54] during charging or [0.13, 0.14] during discharging. When the battery voltage reaches [4.02, 4.14] during charging or drops to [3.41, 3.46] during discharging, the SoC of the battery can best reflect the battery state, as shown in Fig. 10(c) and (d). Additionally, the IC value of the battery best reflects the state of the battery when the voltage reaches the [3.93, 3.99] range during charging or drops to the [3.49, 3.54] range during discharging, as shown in Fig. 10(e) and (f).

The most common method for extracting battery point features relies on statistical information. For example, the extreme and maximum values under a complete charge and discharge cycle are selected as features [23], or information such as the SoC when charging to the constant current charging cutoff voltage is designated as features [21]. There are two limitations with these methods: first, feature extraction methods based on statistical information require complete charge/discharge data; second, the validity of these features is limited by human experience. Therefore, this subsection uses the optimal feature index obtained from the Bayesian optimization search to extract features and compares them with these methods to verify the effectiveness of the proposed feature extraction method.

In this experiment, the maximum IC value during constant-current discharging and the SoC value at the end of constant-current charging are selected as the baseline features for comparison with traditional methods. These are compared with the corresponding features under the feature index values determined by the Bayesian optimization search in this study, i.e., the IC value at 3.51 V discharge voltage and the SoC at 4.09 V charge voltage. The results are shown in Fig. 11 , where the feature index determined by the Bayesian optimization algorithm can successfully extract features that are comparable to or even exceed the point features that rely on expert experience. In the figure, the black curve indicates the actual SOH of the battery, the blue curve indicates the estimated SOH based on the features extracted by the traditional method, and the orange curve indicates the estimated SOH based on the features from the Bayesian optimization results. From the experiments, it can be found that the method of selecting the features under specific feature indexes is easier to implement than the method of selecting the features that rely on statistical information such as the maximum value. This is because this method does not have the need to collect a large amount of data from the complete loop to reduce statistical bias, which improves the efficiency and accuracy of feature extraction.

For interval features of battery data, determining the interval size is critical to the feature validity. If the interval is too large, redundant features will be introduced; if the interval is too small, valid information may be lost. To verify the capability of the Bayesian method proposed in this paper in extracting feature intervals characterizing battery aging information, this subsection compares the performance difference in feature interval extraction between several traditional methods based on expert experience and the Bayesian optimization method proposed.

This paper first compare the difference between the area of constant-current charging area using Voltage-SoC curves and the area of Voltage-SoC curves characterized by BO search in the voltage interval of 4.02–4.14 V. In addition, the difference between the voltage range of 4.02–4.14 V obtained by BO search and the isovoltage drop time as a characteristic of constant-current charging was compared using the charging voltage range of 3.5–4.2 V in Ref. [33] was compared, where the isovoltage drop represents the time required for the battery to change from a certain voltage to another voltage in different cycles. The results are shown in Fig. 12 , from which it can be seen that the SOH of the battery estimated by the extraction method of interval features based on Bayesian optimization proposed in this paper is more in line with the actual SOH decay trend.

We also compare the method of extracting the area of the part of the IC curve as a feature in Ref. [34] and characterize the aging capability of the battery by comparing the area of 50, 100, and 150 mV regions near the peak of the IC curve in the case of constant-current discharging as an interval feature and the area of the IC curve in the voltage interval 3.54–3.49 V obtained by BO search as an interval feature. The results are shown in Fig. 13 , from which it can be seen that the changes in battery capacity estimated by the feature interval obtained by the BO search are smoother and the trend is obvious.

The third comparative experiment conducted a detailed evaluation of the effectiveness of features obtained by using different interval data as inputs to dimensionality reduction algorithms. The analysis involved comparing results from both linear dimensionality reduction techniques, such as Principal Component Analysis (PCA), and nonlinear methods like Local Tangent Space Alignment (LTSA). Considering that directly using time as an index, as done in Ref. [32], lacks universality, this study opted to use voltage data within a fixed State of Charge (SoC) interval as the input for dimensionality reduction algorithms.

In the empirical feature extraction approach, the complete charging voltage data was used as input, while the method guided by Bayesian Optimization (BO) utilized voltage data with SoC ranging from 0.47 to 0.54 as input. The experimental results are shown in Fig. 14 . As can be seen in the figure, using PCA to reduce the voltage data within the interval to a scalar feature almost perfectly matches the aging trend of battery capacity. Moreover, using only a portion of the voltage data within the interval obtained by the BO search achieves, and even surpasses, the effect obtained using the complete data, significantly reducing computational costs. In contrast, when using LTSA, the differences between the two curves are very pronounced. The main reason for this is that when employing manifold learning dimensionality reduction methods like LTSA, the sampling frequency of the input data should be equal. Since the sampling frequencies of data within the same interval vary across different cycles, resampling is required based on time information. Resampling is highly inefficient when the data are rapidly changing, unevenly distributed, or non-smooth.

Therefore, for both LTSA and PCA, which are model-free dimensionality reduction algorithms, using intervals obtained by BO search to extract features yields robust results. Moreover, the results obtained using a small amount of data within the narrow interval found by the BO search are comparable to those obtained using the complete data. This validates that the intervals obtained by the BO search can effectively capture battery aging features while significantly reducing computational complexity.

Finally, the heat map shown in Fig. 15 is drawn using six features and their comparison features, where different rows represent different features, the top six features are extracted using the index or interval searched in this paper, while the bottom six features are extracted via corresponding empirical methods. Different columns represent different correlation indicators, the first column represents the Pearson correlation coefficient between features and battery capacity, and the second column represents the information coefficient between features and battery capacity.

Specifically, in Fig. 15, A and A′ denote the IC value when the battery voltage drops to 3.51 V during discharge and the maximum IC value during the discharge process, respectively. B and B′ represent the SoC when the voltage rises to 4.06 V during charging and the SoC at the end of constant current charging, respectively. C and C′ describe the area under the voltage-SoC curve during the voltage interval from 4.02 to 4.14 V and throughout the entire constant current charging process, respectively. D and D′ indicate the time required for voltage changes within the intervals from 4.02 to 4.14 V and from 3.5 to 4.2 V during charging, respectively. E and E′ refer to the area enclosed by the current curve within the voltage interval from 3.54 to 3.49 V during discharge and within a 50 mV range around the peak current, respectively; F and F’ denote the voltage when the SoC is between 0.47 and 0.54 during charging and the characteristics obtained from the entire charging process voltage as PCA input, respectively. Correlation analysis shows that these feature intervals extracted by the Bayesian optimization method have a high correlation with battery capacity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ensemble robust model to estimate SOH
ce:section.ce:para: In order to ensure the validity of the model under different data distributions, this section adopts a cross-validation approach, and the division method is shown in Table 3 . The data are divided into multiple folds, which are alternately used as the training set and test set for model training and evaluation, thus reducing the influence of chance due to a single data partition. This division method not only can effectively reduce the risk of model overfitting but also ensure that the model has high stability and prediction accuracy.

To verify the validity of the appeal features and the superiority of the model proposed in this paper, we performed SOH estimation using the model proposed in this paper on the features extracted by the method in this paper and the empirically based features, respectively, and the results are shown in Fig. 16 .

Specifically, the six BO result-oriented features and the six corresponding traditional features in Fig. 15 are input into the integrated robust model of this paper, and the results in Fig. 16 are obtained. The experimental results show that the features extracted using the method in this paper are more favorable for estimating SOH and more robust, and the estimated curve is smoother and closer to the trend of the real capacity.

The absolute errors (AE) estimated using the two types of features are shown in Fig. 17 . Although the difference between the errors is not significant on batteries B05 and B07, it can be seen from the absolute error subplot in Fig. 16 that the use of empirically extracted features based on the experience is more susceptible to the influence of noise and other influences, and the prediction is more oscillatory.

In order to verify the superiority of this paper's model, we conducted ablation experiments and comparison experiments, in which the point features and interval features extracted by this paper's method were input into the integrated robust linear model proposed in this paper as well as other kinds of state-of-the-art regression models for SoH estimation, and the results of the experiments are shown in Table 4 .

In the ablation experiments, this paper compares the two base models (BRR regression and RANSAC regression) used in the integrated model of this paper, respectively, and in the comparison experiments, we compare Support Vector Regression (SVR), Random Forest, Light Gradient Boosting Machine(LightGBM), Multi-layer Perceptron (MLP), and Long Short-Term Memory Networks(LSTM), and compare the performance of five mainstream machine learning and deep learning models with the estimated SoH on four independent test sets (B0005, B0006, B0007, and B0018). The results show that the integrated robust model proposed in this paper exhibits excellent prediction ability in all test sets. For Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and Root Mean SquareError (RMSE), which are two key measures of prediction error, the integrated robust model generally achieves the lowest values, indicating that its predicted battery capacity has the smallest gap with the true value. In addition, for the R2 score, a measure of the model's goodness-of-fit, the integrated robust model achieves the highest scores in the B0005 and B0018 test sets, which means that the model explains and captures the trends of the data well on these datasets.

Regarding the ablation experiments, although the performance of the BRR and RANSAC variants on some of the test sets was slightly lower than that of the fully integrated robust model, it still demonstrates the validity of the model's internal structure and methodology. In the comparison experiments, the integrated robust model significantly outperforms the comparison models SVR, Random Forest, LightGBM, MLP, and LSTM in most cases. It is worth noting that in the B0006 and B0018 test sets, the R2 scores of the LSTM and MLP models show non-normal values, implying that these two models may not be able to effectively capture the pattern of battery capacity variation on these two specific datasets, and the activation function in the MLP may have degraded the performance of the model due to the extracted features being highly linearized, which may be more important in the case of LSTM, and the activation function in the MLP may have degraded the performance of the model. LSTM may be more suitable for time-series prediction scenarios than for the regression prediction task of predicting the capacity under the corresponding cycle using features extracted from within the cycle.

In this paper, we innovatively introduce the cycle scale to analyze battery aging from the perspective of the whole life cycle and provide a novel approach for battery feature extraction. A feature evaluation metric is designed based on the volatility of features and their correlation with capacity. In particular, a general framework is constructed for searching the most efficient battery feature indexes. This framework utilizes Bayesian Optimization to identify the optimal feature indexes and can be used for different combinations of features and feature indexes. Furthermore, an ensemble robust linear model is developed. Experiments show that the model achieves the best results on all of the batteries and features.

Xiaolong Chen: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology. Huihui Yang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Yingze Yang: Supervision, Conceptualization. Heng Li: Supervision, Project administration. Yuan Chen: Investigation. Chaolong Zhang: Investigation. Longxing Wu: Conceptualization. Lisen Yan: Investigation.
The data and materials used to support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52377221, 62172448), and the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (No. 2023JJ30698).
